text: problem (ibid.). 3 Even Andrew Lang, in his otherwise critical review published in Man (van Gennep had criticized Lang’s approach quite extensively), had to admit that as a corpus on Australian myths and legends, the book held great value (Lang 1906: 126). Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 51 Very fundamentally, van Gennep puts his finger on Durkheim’s reductionist stance which simplified everything as a need and function of the primordial social entity called ‘society’. But here he raises the stakes, evidencing the connections to the not-so-unrelated political ideologisms that so easily follow from almost any strongly argued collectivist theory: for what is lurking behind this sociologism is something even deeper and more problematic, and goes way beyond the classification of Australian kinship. Van Gennep returns to Durkheim and sums up his critique on p. XXXV, in a subsection on ‘Social Modifications’, in a paragraph that is worth quoting in full: We have seen how M. Durkheim explains social modifications by the ‘needs of society’ without indicating neither the why nor the where of those needs, and without justifying how exactly a ‘society’, however small, may have ‘needs’ in the first place. It is by an identical process of animation that they speak to us of ‘the call of the fatherland’, or ‘the voice of the race’. M. Durkheim anthropomorphizes, even if this is what he pretends to defend himself from. (emphasis and inverted commas in the original, my translation) 4 According to van Gennep, Durkheim’s sociology was not just flawed at the theoretical level; the entire epistemology onto which it built bore resemblances to other and much more serious political essentialisms. Here it must be stressed very explicitly that van Gennep was of course not implying that Durkheim was ‘racist’ or ‘nationalist’ in any direct or simplistic meaning of those terms. Durkheim had strongly condemned racism and anti-Semitism, and certainly not only because of his own Jewish background. 5 Something else was at stake, and it concerned the appreciation of concrete living human beings, acting within the limits of their social and physical environments. Van Gennep argued that Durkheim’s categorical collectivism lost sight of real human beings. When Gabriel Tarde was still alive, he called this Durkheim’s tendency to dangerously construct society as a ‘divine Being’, sacrificing the individual at its altar. Durkheim now found himself with a new opponent, re-launching Tarde’s critique from another angle. And this was not just any opponent: it was someone who knew the ethnographic material onto which Durkheim had projected to construct his universalist theories of religion far better than himself. But he was also much younger and held no position. Durkheim answered van Gennep with silence; but there can be very little 4 This passage is evidently mistranslated in Zumwalt’s article from 1982, and is therefore quite important to get right, as it is possibly the earliest critique of Durkheim’s work on religion that exists. 5 The question of nationalism is more complex. As we know, Durkheim became an ardent and patriotic defender of the French Republic during the Great War. As Dingley (2008: 42) argued, in Durkheim’s works, ‘the individual was a social reality, the product of a society that was itself a reality, and for Durkheim the implicit reality of society was the nation, particularly the French nation’. Liminality and the Modern 52 doubt that he discussed the critique with Mauss. It is likely that he left it for Mauss to deal with. Arnold van Gennep’s 1913 Review of The Elementary Forms of Religious Life Van Gennep did not waste his time just discussing Durkheim; most of what he published after 1906 consisted of constructive attempts to make sense of a variety of ethnographic data, including ritual forms and passages as discussed in Rites of Passage. His many book reviews written in that period would always try to distil whatever could be said about somebody else’s work that was positive. But when Durkheim published EFRL van Gennep simply could not keep quiet. And by then he also had little reason to do so: he was by now working in Switzerland, and at a safe distance from French academic power struggles. Or, so he thought. EFRL is in fact the one book that more than any other should have discussed Rites of Passage, and for several reasons. Durkheim’s most important anthropological work was written during the period when van Gennep’s work had been made public. Mauss, who did much (some say most) of the work for EFRL, had closely followed van Gennep’s work during the year-long preparation of the material. Just like van Gennep, Durkheim famously singled out ritual as central to the constitution of not only religion but also society itself. However, Durkheim made no use of the terms or distinctions suggested in Rites de Passage. Van Gennep is dismissively (a ‘drawback’) mentioned in a footnote to Book II, Chapter 2, with a reference to an article he published in 1908 on totemism and the comparative method in Revue de l’Histoire des Religions (Durkheim 1995: 136). Van Gennep’s book Tabou et Totemisme is mentioned (but not discussed) once, in a footnote to Book III, Chapter 1 (ibid.: 324) as one of several works that document the contagiousness of the sacred. Durkheim does not consider how van Gennep further developed this quite crucial point about contagion in his larger classification of rites undertaken in Rites of Passage. When Durkheim in Book II goes into some detail on the myths and legends that underpin Australian totemic beliefs, Mythes et légendes d’Australie. Etude d’ethnographie et de sociologie by van Gennep – a whole book dedicated to the ethnographic case study that grounds the entire argument of EFRL – is not once referred to. In this founding work of sociology, Durkheim does not find it worthwhile even to discuss van Gennep, possibly the person in France who knew most about the substance of EFRL. Why not?
